name: न्यायिक कृत्रिम बुद्धिमत्ता: 'एआई जज' (AI Judge)
description: 'एआई जज' (AI Judge) या न्यायिक कृत्रिम बुद्धिमत्ता का तात्पर्य उन उन्नत प्रणालियों से है जो न्यायिक कार्यों में योगदान देती हैं या न्यायाधीश की भूमिका को पूरी तरह प्रतिस्थापित करने की क्षमता रखती हैं। वर्तमान में न्याय प्रणाली सहायक तकनीकों (जैसे दस्तावेज़ों की ई-फाइलिंग) से आगे बढ़कर 'प्रतिस्थापन' (Replacement) और 'विघटनकारी' (Disruptive) तकनीकों की ओर विकसित हो रही है। इसके वैश्विक उदाहरण भी दिखने लगे हैं; जैसे चीन में 'स्मार्ट कोर्ट' पहल के तहत एआई न्यायाधीशों को समान मामलों के सुझाव देता है और 'असामान्य निर्णयों' के प्रति सचेत करता है, वहीं एस्टोनिया छोटे दावों के निपटारे के लिए 'रोबोट जज' विकसित करने की योजना पर काम कर रहा है।न्यायिक प्रक्रिया में एआई का समावेश जहाँ दक्षता और सटीकता बढ़ा सकता है, वहीं यह 'मानवीय तत्व' (Human Element) के भविष्य पर भी गंभीर सवाल खड़े करता है। न्याय केवल डेटा का विश्लेषण नहीं है, बल्कि इसमें सहानुभूति, सामान्य ज्ञान और मानवीय आचरण की बारीकियों को समझना शामिल है, जिसे मशीनें वर्तमान में पूरी तरह नहीं अपना सकतीं। इसके अतिरिक्त, एल्गोरिदम में 'एल्गोरिथमिक पक्षपात' (Algorithmic Bias) का खतरा और 'ब्लैक बॉक्स' जैसी अपारदर्शिता पारदर्शिता के सिद्धांतों को चुनौती देती है, जो अंततः न्यायिक स्वतंत्रता और कानून के शासन पर प्रभाव डाल सकती है

name: सोशल मीडिया और जजों की निष्पक्षता
description: आज हम चर्चा करने जा रहे हैं एक ऐसे विषय पर जो न्यायपालिका की विश्वसनीयता, अभिव्यक्ति की स्वतंत्रता और डिजिटल युग की चुनौतियों के बीच एक महत्वपूर्ण प्रश्न खड़ा करता है—"सोशल मीडिया और जजों की निष्पक्षता"। सोशल मीडिया ने संवाद और विचारों के आदान-प्रदान को पहले से कहीं अधिक आसान बना दिया है। लेकिन जब न्यायाधीश जैसे संवैधानिक पदों पर आसीन व्यक्ति इस डिजिटल दुनिया का हिस्सा बनते हैं, तो यह सवाल उठता है कि उनकी ऑनलाइन गतिविधियाँ न्यायिक निष्पक्षता की सार्वजनिक धारणा को किस प्रकार प्रभावित कर सकती हैं। इसी संदर्भ में हम समझेंगे "बैंगलोर प्रिंसिपल्स ऑफ ज्यूडिशियल कंडक्ट" को—वे अंतरराष्ट्रीय मानक जो न्यायाधीशों के आचरण के लिए स्वतंत्रता, निष्पक्षता, सत्यनिष्ठा, शिष्टाचार, समानता और दक्षता जैसे मूल्यों को स्थापित करते हैं। क्या सोशल मीडिया पर व्यक्त किए गए विचार किसी न्यायाधीश की निष्पक्षता पर प्रश्नचिह्न लगा सकते हैं? क्या डिजिटल युग में न्यायिक गरिमा और सार्वजनिक विश्वास को बनाए रखने के लिए नए मानदंडों की आवश्यकता है? इन्हीं महत्वपूर्ण सवालों पर होगी आज की गहन चर्चा। तो बने रहिए हमारे साथ।

name: न्यायिक साहस
description: न्यायिक साहस (Judicial Courage) न्यायपालिका का एक अत्यंत महत्वपूर्ण गुण है। इसका अर्थ है कि न्यायाधीश किसी भी प्रकार के राजनीतिक, सामाजिक, आर्थिक या व्यक्तिगत दबाव से प्रभावित हुए बिना निष्पक्ष और न्यायसंगत निर्णय लेने का साहस रखें। न्यायिक साहस कानून के शासन (Rule of Law) और लोकतंत्र की मजबूती का आधार है।

name: Quie Timet Actions in Public Law
description: This is to understand what quie timet actions are and next how do they apply to public law litigations.

name: जजों की निष्पक्षता न्यायपालिका की विश्वसनीयता का आधार है
description: जजों की निष्पक्षता न्यायपालिका की विश्वसनीयता का आधार है। बैंगलोर सिद्धांत (Bangalore Principles of Judicial Conduct) न्यायाधीशों के आचरण हेतु छह मूल्यों—स्वतंत्रता, निष्पक्षता, सत्यनिष्ठा, शिष्टता, समानता तथा दक्षता एवं परिश्रम—पर बल देते हैं। ये सिद्धांत न्यायिक निर्णयों में पारदर्शिता, निष्पक्षता और जनविश्वास बनाए रखने में महत्वपूर्ण भूमिका निभाते हैं।

name: World War II and Emergency in India - What is not the rule of law
description: Understanding and correlating two crisis of the last century.

name: Thou Doth Reason too much - Discussing Role of Reasoning Article by Professor Talia Fisher
description: Today we are diving into a provocative and counter-intuitive piece from the Cambridge Law Review (2025) titled, 'Within the Sound of Silence: Reassessing the Role of Reasoning in Judicial Decision-Making' by Professor Talia Fisher. In the world of law, we often treat the idea that judges must provide detailed reasons for their rulings as an absolute truth—an 'unexamined background assumption'. We rely on these reasons to ensure transparency, build legal precedents, and protect the autonomy of the people in court. However, Professor Fisher argues that this universal expectation might actually be self-defeating in certain scenarios. In this episode, we’ll explore the controversial 'shadow docket' of the US Supreme Court. We will also discuss the fascinating psychological concept of 'verbal overshadowing,' where the act of putting a decision into words can actually distort the judge's accuracy, particularly when assessing witness credibility. We’ll also look at 'hard cases' like Bush v. Gore , where the court’s attempt to explain itself risked creating 'bad law'. Finally, we’ll ask: Can strategic silence actually be a better tool for democracy than a 50-page written opinion?. Stay tuned as we reassess the limits of reasoned judgments."

name: गिरफ्तारी के समय वकील तक पहुँच का अधिकार
description: आज हम एक ऐसे विषय पर चर्चा करेंगे जो हर नागरिक की व्यक्तिगत स्वतंत्रता और उनके कानूनी अधिकारों की नींव है— गिरफ्तारी के समय वकील तक पहुँच का अधिकार क्सर लोग सोचते हैं कि यह सिर्फ फिल्मों की बात है, लेकिन अंतरराष्ट्रीय स्तर पर ICCPR (International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights) जैसे कानून स्पष्ट करते हैं कि हर व्यक्ति को अपनी पसंद का वकील चुनने और उनसे गोपनीयता के साथ बात करने का पूरा हक है।भारत में, हमारे संविधान का अनुच्छेद 22 (Article 22) यह सुनिश्चित करता है कि किसी भी गिरफ्तार व्यक्ति को उसकी गिरफ्तारी के कारणों (grounds) के बारे में बताए बिना हिरासत में नहीं रखा जाएगा। हाल ही में, विहान कुमार बनाम हरियाणा राज्य (Vihaan Kumar v. State of Haryana) के ऐतिहासिक मामले में सुप्रीम कोर्ट ने इस अधिकार को और भी मजबूती दी है।अदालत ने यह स्पष्ट किया है कि गिरफ्तारी के कारणों को लिखित रूप में और ऐसी भाषा में बताना अनिवार्य है जिसे गिरफ्तार व्यक्ति समझ सके। यह सिर्फ एक औपचारिकता नहीं है, बल्कि इसलिए ज़रूरी है ताकि वह व्यक्ति प्रभावी कानूनी सलाह (effective counsel) प्राप्त कर सके और अपनी जमानत के लिए सही तरीके से पक्ष रख सके। अगर इन नियमों का पालन नहीं होता, तो वह गिरफ्तारी ही अवैध (vitiated) मानी जा सकती है।तो चलिए, आज के इस एपिसोड में हम गहराई से समझेंगे कि अंतरराष्ट्रीय मानक और भारतीय कानून मिलकर कैसे आपकी सुरक्षा करते हैं और 'विहान कुमार' केस ने गिरफ्तारी की प्रक्रिया में क्या बड़े बदलाव किए हैं।क्या आप चाहेंगे कि मैं इस विषय पर आपके लिए कुछ फ्लैशकार्ड्स तैयार करूँ ताकि आप इन महत्वपूर्ण कानूनी अवधारणाओं को बेहतर ढंग से याद रख सकें?

name: Burden of Proof and Citizenship: Carrot-Stick and a sharp weapon
description: In this episode we discuss legal and social implications of citizenship , criminalisation , and the burden of proof within modern justice systems. One primary focus is the weaponisation of nationality , where states use the revocation of citizenship as a punitive tool against individuals or marginalised groups. Other texts evaluate how statutory exceptions in countries like Australia and India can reverse the onus of proof , forcing defendants to establish their own innocence in matters of terrorism, drug offences, and migration. This creates a significant tension with the presumption of innocence , a fundamental human right designed to prevent the wrongful conviction of the blameless. Furthermore, the materials highlight how state interventions intended to protect victims—particularly in cases of domestic violence—can inadvertently lead to the punishment and misidentification of the very women they aim to support. Ultimately, the collection argues for a balancing act that weighs effective law enforcement against the necessity of protecting individual liberties from arbitrary state power

name: Legal Notes on Citizenship Cases - Foreigners Tribunals in Assam
description: Assam's Foreigners Tribunals: A Legal Handbook on Preliminary Objections Imagine receiving a notice one day questioning your citizenship—the very identity you've lived with your entire life suddenly under scrutiny. For thousands in Assam, this isn't imagination but reality. This handbook, prepared by a legal clinic, serves as a critical resource for advocates practicing before Assam's Foreigners Tribunals (FT). It addresses one of India's most sensitive legal challenges: determining who belongs and who doesn't in a state still grappling with migration anxieties dating back decades. At its core, the guide focuses on preliminary objections—the first line of defense before the tribunals even examine the merits of citizenship claims. These procedural challenges are crucial because they test whether the entire proceeding against an individual was initiated lawfully. Was the investigation conducted fairly? Did authorities follow due process before branding someone a suspected foreigner? Were the references (notices) to the tribunal sent with proper application of mind, or were they mechanical, arbitrary exercises? The handbook grounds its strategy in Article 21 of the Indian Constitution—the right to life and personal liberty—which the Supreme Court has interpreted to include fair procedure before the state strips anyone of their citizenship status. Drawing from judgments across High Courts and the Supreme Court, it equips lawyers to challenge flawed investigations and ill-conceived references that could unjustly render citizens stateless. This isn't just legal technicality—it's about protecting fundamental rights in proceedings where the stakes couldn't be higher.

name: सोशल मीडिया टेकडाउन ऑर्डर: भारतीय कानून की पहेली
description: सोशल मीडिया टेकडाउन ऑर्डर: भारतीय कानून की पहेली क्या सरकार को ऑनलाइन कंटेंट हटाने का अधिकार होना चाहिए? भारत में IT Act की धारा 69A और IT Rules 2021 इस सवाल के केंद्र में हैं। एक तरफ राष्ट्रीय सुरक्षा, सार्वजनिक व्यवस्था और fake news की चुनौतियाँ हैं। दूसरी ओर अभिव्यक्ति की स्वतंत्रता (Article 19(1)(a)) का मौलिक अधिकार खड़ा है। सरकारी आदेश अक्सर गोपनीय रहते हैं, प्लेटफ़ॉर्म्स को compliance करनी पड़ती है, और यूजर्स को पता भी नहीं चलता कि उनका content क्यों हटाया गया। Shreya Singhal judgment ने पारदर्शिता की मांग की, पर क्या implementation पर्याप्त है? आज की चर्चा में हम जानेंगे: कौन-से मामले court के लिए उचित हैं और कौन-से administrative discretion की मांग करते हैं?

name: Understanding Bad Legal Writing and Avoiding being slapped
description: In today’s episode, we examine why bad legal writing is more than a stylistic flaw. Drawing from the work of Heidi K. Brown, we explore how judges view poorly drafted briefs, boilerplate arguments, and careless “cut-and-paste” advocacy as obstacles to justice itself. The discussion highlights how weak pleadings burden courts, damage credibility, and sometimes even invite judicial sanctions. At the heart of the conversation is a simple but powerful idea: legal writing is not merely technical work. It is an ethical responsibility. Clear, disciplined, and intellectually honest advocacy helps courts deliver justice, while careless drafting undermines the judicial process.

name: When Your Post Vanishes: Understanding Takedown Orders in India
description: You wake up to find your social media post has disappeared—no warning, no explanation, just gone. Welcome to the world of content takedown orders in India, where the intersection of free speech and digital regulation creates one of modern law's most contested battlegrounds. Under Section 69A of the IT Act and the Information Technology Rules 2021, the Indian government wields significant power to order the removal of online content. The stated reasons are compelling: national security threats, public order concerns, preventing communal violence, and combating misinformation. But here's the catch—these orders are typically confidential, intermediaries must comply within tight timelines, and users often never learn why their content was removed or who ordered its removal. The legal framework operates in a peculiar shadow zone. While the Supreme Court's Shreya Singhal judgment (2015) struck down vague provisions and demanded procedural safeguards, questions persist about transparency, accountability, and the balance between legitimate state interests and fundamental rights under Article 19(1)(a).

name: "द ग्रामर ऑफ लिटिगेशन" (वकालत का व्याकरण)
description: हम लॉ स्कूल में कानून की किताबें और सिद्धांत तो पढ़ लेते हैं, लेकिन किताबी ज्ञान (थ्योरी) और अदालत की असल दुनिया (प्रैक्टिस) के बीच एक बहुत बड़ा फासला होता है। लॉ स्कूल आपको यह सिखा सकता है कि कानून की बुनियादी बातें क्या हैं और उसे कहाँ खोजना है, लेकिन अदालत के भीतर जज के सामने किस बात को कैसे और कब रखना है, यह आपको केस लॉज़ और खुद के अनुभव से सीखना पड़ता है तो चलिए, बिना किसी देरी के शुरू करते हैं वकालत के सफर और उसके असली व्याकरण को समझने की यह खास चर्चा!

name: Samurai's Code for Lawyers - Discussion on Kanemoto's article
description: In today’s episode, we explore an unusual but deeply compelling idea: what can modern lawyers learn from the ethical code of the samurai? Drawing from the work of Chenise S. Kanemoto, this discussion examines how the ancient Japanese philosophy of Bushido may offer a path out of the modern crisis of legal professionalism. At a time when many lawyers experience burnout, disillusionment, and growing public distrust in the legal system, Kanemoto argues that technical competence alone is not enough. Rules of professional conduct may define the minimum standards of behaviour, but they do not necessarily cultivate character. Bushido, by contrast, demands an internal discipline rooted in honour, humility, courage, restraint, and respect. The episode unpacks how these seemingly abstract virtues translate into everyday legal practice: the discipline to prepare honestly, the humility to acknowledge weakness in an argument, the restraint to maintain civility in adversarial settings, and the integrity to place justice above ego. From courtroom etiquette to client communication, the conversation reflects on whether lawyering should be viewed not merely as a profession, but as a moral craft. At its core, this is a meditation on character in the administration of justice. Can a lawyer remain fierce without becoming cruel? Can advocacy coexist with humility? And in an age of increasing polarization and performative litigation, does the legal profession need not just sharper minds, but steadier moral anchors?

name: Courage of a Lawyer: Ethical Imagination of legal professional
description: In today’s episode, we explore a question that lies at the moral core of the legal profession: what separates a competent lawyer from a truly courageous one? Drawing from the reflections of John Cannon Few, this conversation examines how moral courage shapes the pursuit of justice across generations. Through the intertwined legacies of Frederick Douglass, Thurgood Marshall, and J. Waties Waring, we trace how acts of principled defiance against racial segregation transformed not only constitutional law, but the ethical imagination of the legal profession itself. These were individuals who chose integrity over acceptance, constitutional fidelity over social comfort, and justice over personal safety. The episode also reflects on how courage travels across time. The willingness of one lawyer or judge to stand against injustice often empowers another generation to do the same. From courtroom advocacy to judicial independence, the law advances because some individuals are prepared to endure criticism, isolation, and retaliation in defence of principle. At its heart, this is not merely a discussion about legal history in the US. It is about the continuing responsibility of lawyers to speak when silence is safer, to resist when conformity is rewarded, and to remember that justice depends not only on knowledge of law, but on the courage to uphold it.

name: Hate Speech in India - Is the present law sufficient? SC Answers
description: Welcome to our podcast! Today, we dive into a landmark Supreme Court of India judgment addressing the critical issue of hate speech. We will explore the Court's ruling on constitutional fraternity, the boundaries of judicial power, and police accountability under existing criminal laws. Let's get started

name: Conditions of work in ISRO and SC Judgment
description: Today, we are diving into the decades-long legal battle of the "Gang Labourers" at ISRO's highly sensitive Mahendragiri facility. These daily-wage workers, engaged as far back as 1991 to do the heavy lifting—loading, unloading, and shifting materials—spent over thirty years fighting for basic job security and permanent status. Despite a previous tribunal order meant to secure their livelihoods, they were kept in temporary roles under a 2012 government scheme.In a powerful and moving ruling, the Supreme Court struck down these temporary conditions. The Court reminded the nation of its humble beginnings—like transporting the first rocket components on a bicycle in Thumba in 1963, and moving the APPLE satellite on a bullock cart in 1981. The Justices declared that the State must act as a "model employer" and finally ordered the permanent regularization of these workers.

name: Stopping Public Harm - Use of "quia timet" injunctions in Public Law
description: Examine the legal principles governing hypothetical, academic, and premature challenges within administrative and constitutional law. Why UK courts generally avoid abstract disputes, noting that judicial resources should be reserved for cases with practical relevance rather than intellectual curiosity. Exploring the tension between judicial review and parliamentary independence, particularly when inaction is viewed as an abdication of constitutional duty

name: Cloudfare and Digital Barriers
description: Discussion on automated security notification generated by Cloudflare

name: Discussion on Thick Skin Bias in judgments about people in Poverty
description: The research identifies a thick skin bias, a psychological phenomenon where people assume those living in poverty are less affected by negative life events than wealthier individuals. This misconception stems from the false belief that persistent hardship toughens the poor, even though chronic stress actually increases emotional and physical vulnerability. Studies involving both laypeople and professionals—such as teachers and social workers—show that this bias applies to adults and children across various ethnicities. Interestingly, while the poor are seen as desensitised to pain, they are simultaneously perceived as being more deeply impacted by positive events than the rich. This social judgment is particularly damaging because it can lead to systemic neglect and a lack of empathy from those providing essential services. Ultimately, the bias justifies inequality by suggesting that those with fewer resources require less care and consideration. Discussion on Cheek, N. N., & Shafir, E. (2024). The thick skin bias in judgments about people in poverty. Behavioural Public Policy, 8 (2), 238–263. https://doi.org/10.1017/bpp.2020.33

name: Defining Rights : Professor Jud Campbell v/s Professor Stephanie Hall Barclay
description: A scholarly debate between Professor Jud Campbell and Professor Stephanie Hall Barclay regarding the historical nature and modern enforcement of constitutional rights. Campbell argues that the Bill of Rights primarily declared pre-existing natural rights rather than creating new legal content, suggesting that these rights were often meant for political determination by legislatures rather than judicial enforcementBased on: https://harvardlawreview.org/forum/vol-138/constructing-constitutional-rights/

name: खामेनेई की हत्या और कूटनीतिक भूचाल
description: नमस्कार श्रोताओं! 'खामेनेई की हत्या और कूटनीतिक भूचाल' पर आधारित हमारे इस खास पॉडकास्ट एपिसोड में आपका स्वागत है।28 फरवरी 2026 को अमेरिका और इज़राइल के संयुक्त सैन्य अभियान, 'ऑपरेशन एपिक फ्यूरी' (Operation Epic Fury), के तहत ईरान के सर्वोच्च नेता अयातुल्ला अली खामेनेई की हत्या ने पूरे मध्य पूर्व को हिला कर रख दिया है। इस अभूतपूर्व घटना ने न केवल ईरान में एक बड़ा राजनीतिक शून्य पैदा कर दिया है, बल्कि अंतरराष्ट्रीय कानून, राष्ट्रों की संप्रभुता और युद्ध के नियमों पर भी एक तीखी वैश्विक बहस छेड़ दी है।कानूनी विशेषज्ञ इस बात पर विचार कर रहे हैं कि क्या बिना किसी आसन्न खतरे के एक संप्रभु राष्ट्र के नेता की इस तरह हत्या करना संयुक्त राष्ट्र चार्टर के अनुच्छेद 2(4) और अंतरराष्ट्रीय मानवाधिकार कानून का खुला उल्लंघन है, या इसे आत्मरक्षा (self-defense) के अधिकार के तहत जायज़ ठहराया जा सकता है।दूसरी तरफ, ईरान ने इसे एक "कायरतापूर्ण आतंकवादी कृत्य" करार देते हुए खाड़ी देशों में मौजूद अमेरिकी ठिकानों और इज़राइल पर भारी जवाबी हमले किए हैं। इस कूटनीतिक भूचाल ने पूरी दुनिया को एक बड़े क्षेत्रीय युद्ध के मुहाने पर लाकर खड़ा कर दिया है।आज के इस एपिसोड में, हम इस ऐतिहासिक घटनाक्रम, इसके पीछे के अंतरराष्ट्रीय कानूनों की पेचीदगियों और मध्य पूर्व के भविष्य पर पड़ने वाले इसके दूरगामी परिणामों का गहराई से विश्लेषण करेंगे। तो आइए, आज की इस अहम चर्चा की शुरुआत करते हैं।

name: Discussion on Book Review : "Fourth Amendment Equilibrium Adjustment in an Age of Technological Upheaval", by ⁠Jennifer Stisa Granick⁠
description: Book Review published in Harvard Law Review "Fourth Amendment Equilibrium Adjustment in an Age of Technological Upheaval", by Jennifer Stisa Granick At: https://harvardlawreview.org/print/vol-139/fourth-amendment-equilibrium-adjustment-in-an-age-of-technological-upheaval/

name: विदेशी धरती पर सैन्य हमलों का कानून
description: यह स्रोत मुख्य रूप से अंतरराष्ट्रीय कानून के तहत गैर-राज्य अभिनेताओं (जैसे आतंकवादी समूहों) के खिलाफ सैन्य बल के उपयोग की वैधता का विश्लेषण करते हैं। इसमें विशेष रूप से 2020 में अमेरिकी ड्रोन हमले में ईरानी जनरल कासिम सुलेमानी की हत्या का उदाहरण देते हुए 'आत्मरक्षा' (self-defense) के दावों और संयुक्त राष्ट्र चार्टर के उल्लंघन पर चर्चा की गई है। पाठ यह जांचता है कि क्या किसी देश की संप्रभुता का उल्लंघन किए बिना दूसरे देश की धरती पर हमला करना कानूनी रूप से जायज है। लेखक यह स्पष्ट करते हैं कि वर्तमान कानून सशस्त्र हमलों के खिलाफ तत्काल प्रतिक्रिया की अनुमति तो देते हैं, लेकिन भविष्य के "आसन्न" खतरों के नाम पर की गई हत्याएं अक्सर इन नियमों पर खरी नहीं उतरतीं। इसके अतिरिक्त, यह शोध इस बात पर विचार करता है कि क्या बदलती वैश्विक परिस्थितियों के कारण प्रथागत अंतरराष्ट्रीय कानून (customary law) में नए मानदंड विकसित हो रहे हैं। अंततः, यह अंतरराष्ट्रीय शांति बनाए रखने के लिए बल प्रयोग के बजाय मानवाधिकारों और कानूनी सहयोग को प्राथमिकता देने का सुझाव देता है।

name: India's Public Anger and Legal Neutrality
description: Today, we dive deep into the "myth of rationality" to uncover how emotions like anger and fear serve as the true, often invisible, architects of our choices. Drawing on the sources, we examine how these feelings operate across vastly different arenas—from the high-pressure sets of Hollywood to the solemn benches of criminal courts.We’ll explore how moderate anger can actually be a tool for excellence, helping film directors trigger their professional expertise and achieve task effectiveness. Conversely, we’ll see how high-intensity fear can lead decision-makers to abandon their goals entirely in favour of personal security. In legal contexts, we’ll discuss why lawyers may be more resilient to emotional distractions than non-experts, and how gender and age shape our sense of empathy. Finally, we turn to the national scale, investigating India’s "dangerous ticking bomb" of Toxic Anger among youth, which threatens to derail narratives of prosperity amidst widespread climate denial. Join us as we learn that our emotions are not just obstacles to be cleared; they are the fuel in the engine of human agency, capable of powering us toward a goal or causing the entire system to overheat if left unmonitored.

name: Discussion on Article : Excited Delirium, Policing, and the Law of Evidence by Osagie K. Obasogie
description: This is a discussion of an article from Harvard Law Review that critiques the use of "excited delirium" as a medical diagnosis, particularly in cases involving deaths in police custody . The author questions how this condition, which lacks scientific consensus and is not recognised by major bodies like the American Psychiatric Association, became so readily accepted in the legal system and forensic pathology . The article explores the dubious origins of the diagnosis, its use in high-profile cases like the deaths of Angelo Quinto and Elijah McClain, and how federal courts have failed to rigorously apply evidentiary standards like the Daubert framework when allowing expert testimony on the topic. The author concludes by offering policy recommendations to prevent the term and similar concepts from being used to absolve law enforcement of responsibility for excessive forceLink to article: https://harvardlawreview.org/print/vol-138/excited-delirium-policing-and-the-law-of-evidence/

name: राजेंद्र बिहारी लाल बनाम राज्य उत्तर प्रदेश- उत्तर प्रदेश अनैध धार्मिक परिवर्तन निषेध अधिनियम, 2021
description: आज हम देश के सर्वोच्च न्यायालय के एक निर्णायक मुक़ाबले में गहराई से उतरते हैं: राजेंद्र बिहारी लाल बनाम राज्य उत्तर प्रदेश में सुप्रीम कोर्ट का 2025 का महत्वपूर्ण निर्णय। यह फैसला अपीलों और रिट याचिकाओं के एक जटिल समूह से संबंधित है, जो कम से कम छह अलग-अलग प्राथमिकी(एफआईआर) को चुनौती देते हैं। विवाद के केंद्र में विवादित उत्तर प्रदेश अनैध धार्मिक परिवर्तन निषेध अधिनियम, 2021 (सामान्यतः U.P. Conversion Act) है। हम 14 अप्रैल 2022 को फतेहपुर स्थित Evangelical Church of India में कथित रूप से हुई सामूहिक धर्मांतरण घटना की जांच कर रहे हैं, जिसने बाद में कई प्राथमिकाओं को जन्म दिया। याचिकाकर्ताओं का तर्क था कि बाद की दर्ज की गई प्राथमिकाएँ — जैसे कि एफआईआर क्रमांक 54, 55 और 60/2023 — रद्द की जानी चाहिए क्योंकि उन्होंने "समानता के परीक्षण" का उल्लंघन किया है, और ये वहीं कथित घटना के लिए दर्ज की गई थीं जो पहले ही एफआईआर क्रमांक 224/2022 में कवर थी। परन्तु इस मामले ने संवैधानिक रूप से महत्वपूर्ण प्रश्न उठाए: असंशोधित धारा 4 के अंतर्गत एफआईआर दर्ज करने के लिए कानूनी रूप से सक्षम कौन है? इसी तरह पूरे जांच की विश्वसनीयता पर भी सवाल उठे—जाँच में स्पष्ट कमियों के कारण, जिनमें साइक्लोस्टाइल्ड/मशीनी प्रतिलिपि गवाह बयान और रिपोर्ट दर्ज करने में महत्वपूर्ण देरी शामिल थी। हम उस कठोर विधिक तर्क का विश्लेषण करेंगे जिसने अंतिम रूप से सुप्रीम कोर्ट को विवाद सुलझाने पर मजबूर किया—जहाँ अनेक एफआईआर खारिज किए गए और व्यक्तिगत स्वायत्तता तथा अंतरात्मा की स्वतंत्रता को सर्वोपरि बताया गया।

name: उत्तर प्रदेश विधि विरुद्ध धर्म संपरिवर्तन प्रतिषेध अधिनियम, 2021
description: आज हम भारत के सबसे विवादास्पद और गहराई से देखे जाने वाले कानूनी मुद्दों में से एक की पड़ताल करने जा रहे हैं: उत्तर प्रदेश विधि विरुद्ध धर्म संपरिवर्तन प्रतिषेध अधिनियम, 2021 (Uttar Pradesh Prohibition of Unlawful Conversion of Religion Act, 2021)।यह अधिनियम, जो 27 नवंबर, 2020 से प्रभावी हुआ, धर्मांतरण को विनियमित करने और अवैध संपरिवर्तन (conversion) पर रोक लगाने के उद्देश्य से लाया गया था, खासकर उन मामलों में जहां धर्मांतरण दुर्व्यपदेशन, बल, अनुचित प्रभाव, प्रलोभन, या विवाह के माध्यम से किया जाता है।लेकिन क्या यह कानून वास्तव में सामाजिक सुरक्षा और धार्मिक स्वतंत्रता की रक्षा करता है, या यह नागरिक अधिकारों पर एक गंभीर प्रतिबंध है?याचिकाकर्ताओं के अनुसार, यह कानून राज्य को दो स्वतंत्र, सहमति देने वाले वयस्कों की निजी पसंद और उनके जीवनसाथी के चुनाव में अनावश्यक हस्तक्षेप करने की अनुमति देता है। उनका तर्क है कि यह अधिनियम निजता, गरिमा और अंतःकरण की स्वतंत्रता के मूलभूत संवैधानिक मूल्यों का उल्लंघन करता है, और प्रभावी रूप से अंतर्धार्मिक विवाहों को आपराधिक बनाता है।यह कानून कुछ कठोर प्रावधानों के कारण विशेष रूप से आलोचना का शिकार रहा है इस पॉडकास्ट में, हम कानून की जटिलताओं, सुप्रीम कोर्ट के पिछले फैसलों और इसके लागू होने के बाद उत्पन्न हुए वास्तविक उदाहरणों पर गहराई से विचार करेंगे। बने रहिए हमारे साथ.

name: Discussing - Unwarranted Warrants: Judicial Review and Systemic Flaws
description: his legal article critically examines the judicial review process for search warrants in the United States, focusing on an empirical study of over 33,000 warrant applications in Utah. The authors reveal that warrant approvals are extremely fast, with a median review time of just three minutes, and nearly always granted, achieving a 98% ultimate approval rate. They highlight that this rapid, high-approval process often means judges may not thoroughly scrutinise warrant applications, potentially compromising Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable searches and seizures. The article uses real-world examples, such as the deaths of Breonna Taylor and Amir Locke, to illustrate the serious consequences of perfunctory warrant review and proposes various reforms, including continued empirical study, policy changes like mandatory prosecutor review, and institutional adjustments to enhance judicial accountability and decision-making. Based on https://harvardlawreview.org/print/vol-138/unwarranted-warrants-an-empirical-analysis-of-judicial-review-in-search-and-seizure/

name: फांसी की सज़ा और न्याय की असली परीक्षा: बलजींदर कुमार उर्फ़ 'काला'
description: नमस्कार, आप सुन रहे हैं एक विशेष एपिसोड — "कहानी जो रह गई अधूरी: सुप्रीम कोर्ट बनाम मृत्युदंड" । यह एपिसोड उस फैसले पर आधारित है जिसमें सुप्रीम कोर्ट ने बलजींदर कुमार उर्फ़ 'काला' को दी गई मृत्युदंड की सज़ा को पलटते हुए उसे बरी कर दिया Judgment is available here: https://www.sci.gov.in/view-pdf/?diary_no=201582024&type=j&order_date=2025-07-16&from=latest_judgements_order

name: भारतीय संविधान में बंधुत्व (Fraternity) : एक उपेक्षित मूल्य
description: इस विशेष पॉडकास्ट में हम बात कर रहे हैं भारतीय संविधान में 'बंधुत्व' (Fraternity) की अवधारणा की। इसमें यह समझाने का प्रयास किया गया है कि "बंधुत्व" संविधान के मूल उद्देश्यों में से एक क्यों है, इसका सामाजिक और कानूनी महत्व क्या है, और यह समानता, स्वतंत्रता और न्याय के साथ किस तरह जुड़ा हुआ है।

name: वक्फ पर वार? - 2025 संशोधन का विश्लेषण (India)
description: यह एक AI आधारित/generated पॉडकास्ट है। आप सभी श्रोताओं का स्वागत है। इस श्रृंखला में हम वक्फ (संशोधन) अधिनियम, 2025 पर चर्चा करेंगे—उसके कानूनी, सामाजिक और संवैधानिक प्रभावों के साथ। यह पॉडकास्ट स्वतः जनित है, यानी इसमें मानव वाणी की बजाय आर्टिफिशियल इंटेलिजेंस की मदद से आवाज़ और विश्लेषण प्रस्तुत किया गया है। तो आइए, सुनते हैं आज का एपिसोड। #Waqf

name: बिहार में मताधिकार पर हमला: विशेष पुनरीक्षण प्रक्रिया की कानूनी पड़ताल
description: 🔹 नमस्कार! आप सुन रहे हैं — "बिहार में मताधिकार पर हमला: विशेष पुनरीक्षण प्रक्रिया की कानूनी पड़ताल"। 🔹 इस पॉडकास्ट में हम बात करेंगे कि कैसे निर्वाचन आयोग की Special Intensive Revision प्रक्रिया ने मतदाता सूची को लेकर गंभीर सवाल खड़े कर दिए हैं।

name: Special Intensive Revision of Electoral Rolls in Bihar
description: Discussion on the Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls in Bihar and ‘fear of NRC’. Why is everyone up in arms as Election Commission of India is conducting a ‘special intensive revision’ of electoral rolls in Bihar.

name: Plane Crash - Compensation Payable to Passengers (Indian perspective)
description: The podcast explores air carrier liability for passenger death/injury under India's Carriage by Air Act 1972 . It details the evolution of international legal frameworks, from the Warsaw Convention to the Montreal Convention 1999, noting India's adoption of MC99's two-tier compensation system . The Mangalore Air Crash serves as a case study illustrating these complex legal and human dimensions. This is AI Generated Podcast.

name: Discussing BOOK REVIEW of Disturbance, public disorder and everyday violence: a review of Moyukh Chatterjee’s composing violence
description: Devansh Shrivastava (15 Apr 2025): Disturbance, public disorder and everyday violence: a review of Moyukh Chatterjee’s composing violence, Social Identities, DOI: 10.1080/13504630.2025.2491403 https://doi.org/10.1080/13504630.2025.2491403 BOOK REVIEW Disturbance, public disorder and everyday violenc e: a review of Moyukh Chatterjee’s composing violence Composing violence: the limits of exposure and the making of minorities, by Moyukh Chatterjee, Durham and London, Duke University Press, 2023, 184 pp., $94.95 (hardcover), ISBN: 978-1-4780-1702-8

name: Immigration Detention Systems - USA vs. Assam
description: Welcome to our podcast, where we delve into the complexities of immigration detention systems in the USA and Assam, India. Today, we'll explore the legal frameworks, oversight mechanisms, and human rights concerns surrounding these systems. In the USA, the legal landscape is shaped by the Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) and landmark Supreme Court rulings such as Zadvydas v. Davis, which limits the duration of detention and provides avenues for judicial review through habeas corpus. Conversely, Assam relies heavily on the Foreigners Act and executive orders, with detention centers often integrated with jails, raising concerns about due process and the humane treatment of detainees. We'll discuss the issue of indefinite detention, particularly pronounced in Assam due to repatriation challenges and statelessness. In the USA, despite prolonged detention concerns, legal safeguards like the Zadvydas ruling offer some relief by challenging detentions when deportation is not foreseeable. Join us as we compare these systems, highlighting the human rights issues and legal intricacies that define the experiences of immigration detainees in both regions.

name: The Law and Lawlessness of U.S. Immigration Detention by Alina Das
description: Here is a discussion on an article by Alina Das critically examines the U.S. immigration detention system, the largest in the world, highlighting the disconnect between existing constitutional, statutory, and administrative law intended to govern detention conditions and the lived realities reported by detained immigrants and watchdog groups. Das argues that a growing number of federal courts are deferring excessively to the political branches (Congress and the Executive) on matters of immigration detention, assuming their sole interest is in exclusion and deportation, thereby weakening the rights framework for detained individuals. The article challenges this judicial assumption by unearthing a more nuanced "civil detention interest" of the political branches, rooted in a historical concern for humane and non-punitive conditions and a legislative distrust of agency management. Das contends that recognizing this civil detention interest should lead to more robust judicial enforcement of the rights of detained immigrants. Discussion on 138 Harv. L. Rev. 1186 (2025) |

name: Decoding Legal Thinking!
description: Ever wondered what it really means to "think like a lawyer"? In this podcast, we break down the fundamentals of legal reasoning—from identifying key laws and analyzing cases to applying legal principles in real-world situations. Join us as we explore the core methodologies behind legal problem-solving, covering topics like common law, statutory interpretation, constitutional law, contracts, and torts. We’ll also dive into influential legal theories—formalism, legal realism, law and economics, critical legal studies, and feminist legal theory—to see how they shape the way justice is interpreted and applied. Whether you're a law student, legal professional, or just curious about the inner workings of the legal system, this podcast will give you the insights you need to navigate legal reasoning like a pro. Stay tuned, and let’s start thinking like a lawyer!

name: Judicial Decision Making - Frameworks
description: This podcast provides a framework for understanding the complexities of judicial decision-making. It covers various theoretical perspectives, factors influencing judges, and psychological aspects of the decision-making process.

name: Fraternity in the Indian Constitution
description: In this podcast we discuss the concept of fraternity within the Indian Constitution and examine its historical context, its relationship with other constitutional values like liberty and equality, and its complex implications for social harmony and justice. The analysis critically assesses judicial interpretations of fraternity, highlighting concerns about its potential misuse to restrict freedoms and reinforce

name: Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization Examining the Decision and Its Implications
description: Welcome to today’s episode, where we delve into the landmark case of Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization, a decision that has dramatically reshaped the legal landscape surrounding abortion rights in the United States. We'll explore three main points to understand the profound implications of this ruling. Firstly, the Dobbs decision claims to uphold democratic principles by returning the issue of abortion to state legislatures and the people's elected representatives. Justice Alito argued that decisions about abortion should be made through democratic processes rather than by the judiciary. However, this narrative overlooks the fact that the argument for democratic deliberation emerged not immediately after Roe v. Wade but through coordinated legal and political efforts designed to challenge Roe and Casey. Secondly, the decision's vision of democracy is critically flawed. The Dobbs majority focused narrowly on state legislatures, ignoring the broader spectrum of democratic institutions and processes, including federal and local constitutional actors. It also failed to account for the complexities of political representation and power, reducing democracy to mere voting while disregarding issues such as campaign finance and true representation in electoral office. Lastly, the Dobbs decision's reliance on historical and traditional interpretations presents significant antidemocratic implications. By binding constitutional rights to a historical context where many Americans were excluded from legal participation, the ruling risks perpetuating past injustices. Moreover, the Court's recent actions to undermine the electoral process further complicate the promise of genuine democratic deliberation and widely desired outcomes. Join us as we unpack these critical points - in simple English - , examining the Dobbs decision's true impact on democracy and the future of abortion rights in America. Note: Discussion is on Dobbs and Democracy

name: Weaponisation of Reverse Burden in Evidence Law
description: Aims to discuss how is reverse burden of proof being used in various laws all around the world.

name: India's Digital Personal Data Protection - Discussion from Administrative Law Perspective
description: The principles of administrative law are highly relevant to India's Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) framework, as they provide a crucial lens through which to understand and assess the powers and responsibilities of the state and other actors involved in the processing of personal data. Key issues: · Administrative law principles are crucial for India's Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) framework. · Regulation of administrative power is necessary to ensure lawful, fair, and reasonable exercise of new powers by the Data Protection Board and Central Government. · The DPDP framework must adhere to the rule of law, avoiding arbitrary decision s and ensuring actions are based in law. · Delegated legislation under the DPDP Act must be reviewed to stay within granted powers and comply with constitutional principles. · Judicial review ensures that the DPDP framework operates within legal limits and protects fundamental rights. · Principles of natural justice require fairness and impartiality in decisions by the Data Protection Board and other administrative bodies. · Control of discretion is essential to prevent misuse of administrative discretion, such as in cross-border data transfer requirements. · Transparency and openness are emphasized, with clear notices to data principals and published details on exercising their rights. · Accountability measures include mandatory Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), audits, and data breach reporting. · Balancing state powe r with individual rights is a key focus, with outlined rights for data principals and obligations for data fiduciaries. · State processing exemptions must be narrowly defined and safeguarded to prevent misuse. · Effective implementation of the DPDP framework depends on adherence to administrative law principles to avoid undue infringement on individual rights or potential abuse of power.

name: Improving Legal Decision Making - For Judges - Thinking Fast and Slow
description: This discussion aims to highlight how our thinking is often influenced by biases and heuristics, and how we can improve our decision-making by being aware of these influences. Focus is on improving decision making for judges

name: Indian Anti-Trust Action against Meta: ₹213.14 crore for anti-competitive practice
description: The Competition Commission of India (CCI) fined Meta ₹213.14 crore for anti-competitive practices. The CCI found that WhatsApp's 2021 privacy policy update, mandating data sharing with other Meta companies, abused its dominant position in the Indian market for OTT messaging apps. This was deemed an unfair condition, forcing users to accept expanded data collection without an opt-out. The CCI also determined that this data sharing created entry barriers for competitors in the online display advertising market. Consequently, Meta was issued cease-and-desist orders and directed to implement behavioural remedies, including restrictions on data sharing for advertising purposes.

name: India's Managed Democracy - Discussion on Tarunabh Khaitan's Paper
description: Discussion on: Killing a Constitution with a Thousand Cuts: Executive Aggrandizement and Party-State Fusion in India by Tarunabh Khaitan , London School of Economics - Law School This paper assesses claims that Indian democracy is threatened by the current government. It establishes a framework analysing accountability mechanisms – electoral, horizontal (through institutions), and discursive – to determine if the government systematically undermines them. The analysis finds the Modi government subtly weakens these mechanisms, justifying actions through managerial and nationalist rhetoric. While opposition exists, the trend suggests a shift towards a controlled democracy favouring the ruling party, unlike the more overt attacks seen in other countries. The paper contrasts this approach with past instances of democratic erosion, highlighting the gradual, systemic nature of the current threat.

name: Discussion on Adani's Indictment in US Courts
description: This is a discussion of Adani's formal accusation in corruption cases in the US courts. The formal accusation highlights how the "business development" of the company used bribery to secure contracts in India, funded by bonds issued in the United States.

name: Controlled by Anger - Poisoning the Decision Making
description: Key Ideas and Facts and in this Podcast "Emotions and Decision Making" (Lerner & Keltner, 2010): Emotions as Drivers of Decision Making: This review article highlights a paradigm shift in decision theory, recognizing emotions as powerful and predictable forces influencing our choices. It moves beyond Herbert Simon's concept of "bounded rationality" by incorporating the crucial role of emotions. "Nobel Laureate Herbert Simon (1967, 1983) launched a revolution in decision theory when he introduced bounded rationality… But… Simon knew his theory would be incomplete until the role of emotion was specified…" Appraisal-Tendency Framework (ATF): This framework posits that each emotion is associated with specific appraisal tendencies. These tendencies, rooted in the cognitive appraisal of a situation, shape how individuals perceive and respond to subsequent events. For instance, anger, characterized by appraisals of certainty and individual control, often leads to optimistic risk assessments and attributions of blame to others. "…anger being defined by a sense of certainty and individual control along with other-responsibility…" Discrete Emotions, Discrete Effects: The ATF challenges the oversimplification of valence-based models. It provides evidence that emotions of the same valence can have drastically different impacts on decision making. For example, disgust and sadness, both negative emotions, produce opposite effects on the endowment effect. "In sum, incidental disgust eliminated the endowment effect, whereas incidental sadness reversed it." AIMD Model: This model expands traditional decision theory by incorporating the influence of emotions on various stages of the decision-making process. Emotions can alter risk perceptions, time preferences, and goal setting, leading to deviations from purely rational choices. "The AIMD adds emotions to this process in two ways… Current emotions can… distort probabilities… increase or decrease discount rates… and set different motivational goals…" "Fuel in the Fire: How Anger Impacts Judgment and Decision-Making" (Litvak & Lerner, 2010): Anger Superiority Effect: This chapter emphasizes the attention-grabbing nature of anger. Research indicates that individuals are faster and more accurate at identifying angry faces compared to other emotional expressions. This suggests the inherent salience of anger as a social cue. "Hansen and Hansen (1988)… have demonstrated the “anger superiority effect,” or the tendency for people to identify angry faces more quickly and accurately than other emotion expressions." Anger as a "Positive" Emotion?: While traditionally classified as negative, anger exhibits characteristics often associated with positive emotions. It can lead to optimism, increased risk-taking, and feelings of control and power. This challenges the simplistic categorization of emotions. "With this evidence in mind, one might wonder whether anger is actually a positive emotion…" Temporal Perspective on Anger: The authors propose that the perceived valence of anger depends on whether one is reflecting on the past or future. Backward reflection on the anger-inducing event often leads to unpleasant feelings. However, forward reflection, focused on potential actions and control, can elicit positive feelings and a sense of reward. "…anger will be experienced as relatively unpleasant and unrewarding when one is reflecting back on the source of the anger but may be experienced as relatively pleasant and rewarding when one is looking toward the future." Cognitive and Motivational Mechanisms: The chapter explores potential mechanisms explaining anger's effects, including associative networks, informational cues, and motivational drivers. These mechanisms offer nuanced explanations for how anger shapes our attention, processing depth, and ultimately, our decisions. "…people’s emotional states directly inform their judgment… The appraisal content of specific emotions also offers information that people may overgeneralize to subsequent novel situations…"

name: Discussions "In Re: Directions in the matter of demolition of structures "
description: Whether accused person's house can be demolished as punitive measure? Discussion on the landmark ruling by the Supreme Court of India regarding the demolition of structures by government authorities. The judgment addresses the right to shelter, the rule of law, and the separation of powers. It outlines guidelines for the demolition of properties, including the requirement for proper notice, a fair hearing, and an appellate process. The Court emphasizes the importance of ensuring that demolitions are not used as a form of collective punishment against families or individuals who may be connected to an accused person.

name: Chandrachud: Discussing how various scholars have evaluated his tenure
description: This AI podcast explores the legacy and tenure of D.Y. Chandrachud, the 50th Chief Justice of India, focusing on both his landmark judgments and criticisms surrounding his administration. Several sources highlight his rulings on significant matters such as the abrogation of Article 370, the Ayodhya land dispute, the decriminalization of adultery and consensual same-sex relations, and the electoral bond scheme. Other sources discuss his handling of administrative tasks such as judicial reforms and the collegium system, which have been criticized for their perceived bias towards the government.

name: Assam's Foreigners Tribunals and the Rule of Law
description: Discussing whether the Assams Foreigners Tribunals are a misfit with the Government? What is the meaning of the rule of law ?

name: "Legal Implications of Israel’s Policies in Occupied Palestinian Territories"
description: Discussing the Advisory Opinion on the legal consequences of Israel's policies and practices in the occupied Palestinian territories, including East Jerusalem.

name: Right to Legal Counsel - A Fair Trial Guarantee
description: Right to counsel of choice is regarded as a fundamental guarantee in a rule of law governed society. This episode asks some questions about that and lets see what the discussions is.

name: The Communal Constitution: Book Review
description: This is a discussion of book review of Mathew John’s India’s Communal Constitution, which argues that India’s constitution has been shaped by historical forces that have led to the prioritization of religious identity over individual rights. The review specifically examines how colonial laws, particularly those concerning personal law, have contributed to this phenomenon. The author, Talha Abdul Rahman, critiques the judicial interpretations of the constitution that have reinforced these communal tendencies and calls for a more nuanced understanding of the relationship between religion, law, and individual autonomy in India.

name: Emotional Intelligence and Better Advocacy
description: Discusssion of Advocate with Feelings by Kendra Brodin https://www.lawpracticetoday.org/article/advocate-with-feeling-practicing-law-with-eq-and-self-regulation/

name: Angry Judges
description: discussion on how anger leads to poor decision making

name: Poor, Poverty, and Legal System
description: Surface level discussions on the problem of access to justice for disadvantaged people in India and the United States. India's arbitration system disproportionately favors wealthy litigants, due to high arbitrator fees and a lack of provisions for pauper proceedings. Arbitration, while touted as a solution to a clogged judicial system, can become a barrier to access to justice for those with limited financial resources

name: Decolonising Criminal Law - New Criminal Laws in India
description: India's proposed new criminal codes reinforce a colonial culture of command and control. Drafts expand police powers, restrict bail options, and perpetuate the colonial-era legal framework despite the passage of time and the advent of a new, independent India. They highlight the lack of transparency in the drafting process, the inclusion of problematic provisions, and the failure to address existing problems in the current system, calling for a reevaluation and a more comprehensive approach to reform.

name: Discussing Talha Abdul Rahman's Article on Foreigners Tribunal in Assam
description: This article by Talha Abdul Rahman examines the Foreigners Tribunals (FTs) in the Indian state of Assam, which are tasked with determining the citizenship status of residents. The article argues that the FTs are fundamentally flawed and do not meet the basic standards of the rule of law. It highlights that FTs were established through executive order rather than legislation, lack transparency in their selection process, and operate with inadequate procedural safeguards. The author further criticizes the FTs for lacking independence, with members facing potential pressure to reach specific outcomes, and for failing to provide adequate legal assistance to those accused of being foreigners. The article concludes by expressing concern about the potential for widespread statelessness if the Assam model of citizenship verification is implemented across India.

name: Discussing Nikita Sonavane's : Deconstructing Police Discretion as Brahminism
description: In this episode, we dive into the complex and often invisible dynamics of police discretion, especially as it intersects with caste in India. This article argues that beyond overt displays of brutality, the subtler, everyday applications of police power sustain caste-based discrimination. Police discretion—shaped by historical and colonial frameworks—continues to uphold a Brahminical social order under the guise of public safety and order. Using the example of excise policing in Madhya Pradesh, it exposes how excise laws disproportionately target individuals from oppressed caste communities, particularly Denotified Tribes (DNTs). Data reveals a pattern of criminalization tied to caste, while personal narratives shed light on the exploitative practices embedded within these systems. This episode challenges listeners to reconsider policing as not just a matter of law enforcement but as a tool that reinforces caste hierarchies in everyday life. Join us as we unpack the socio-legal layers behind this pressing issue. Article Citation: Nikita Sonavane, 'Deconstructing Police Discretion as Brahminism' (2023) 19(1) Socio-Legal Review 52. https://doi.org/10.55496/PXZR3368

name: Exorcising Dissent: Expulsion of Mohua - Some aspects
description: In today’s episode, we dive into the expulsion of Mahua Moitra from Parliament, following vague allegations in a cash-for-questions scandal. Her case spotlights the ethical standards expected of our elected officials and raises pressing concerns about accountability in Indian politics. Tune in as we break down the implications of this unprecedented decision and what it means for the future of parliamentary integrity

name: Foreign Policy, International (Arms) Trade with Israel: Lens of Judicial Review
description: In Sept, 2024, the Indian Supreme Court dismissed a PIL seeking to revoke arms export licenses to Israel, citing non-interference in foreign affairs. Petitioners argued India’s obligations under the Genocide Convention were at stake. Our podcast critiques the Court’s stance, discussing alternative judicial paths that balance foreign policy with India’s international duties.

name: Algorithms and Fairness
description: Algorithmic Fairness. Challenges in designing fair algorithm

name: CAA and Equality
description: We simplify and discuss on Equality, Arbitrariness and the CAA Farrah Ahmed (2020): Arbitrariness, subordination and unequal citizenship, Indian Law Review, DOI 10.1080/24730580.2020.176114
